explanation: Abram pursued the captors as far as the city of Dan (Genesis 14:14).

prompt: How many kings fought against five in the Valley of Siddim?
correct_answer: Four
explanation: The text describes four kings against five in the battle (Genesis 14:9).

prompt: How many trained men born in his house did Abram lead to rescue Lot?
explanation: Abram led out 318 trained men to pursue the captors (Genesis 14:14).

prompt: What did Melchizedek bring out to Abram when they met?
correct_answer: Bread and wine
explanation: Melchizedek brought out bread and wine to refresh Abram (Genesis 14:18).

prompt: What portion of everything did Abram give to Melchizedek?
correct_answer: A tenth
explanation: Abram gave Melchizedek a tenth (tithe) of all the spoils (Genesis 14:20).

prompt: Where was Lot living when he was taken captive by the four kings?
correct_answer: Sodom
explanation: Lot lived in Sodom at the time the city was plundered (Genesis 14:12).

prompt: Which relative of Abram was taken captive during the war?
correct_answer: His nephew
explanation: Lot, the son of Abram's brother (his nephew), was taken captive (Genesis 14:12).

prompt: Who was the king of Elam who led the alliance of four kings?
correct_answer: Chedorlaomer
explanation: Chedorlaomer was the king of Elam and the leader whom the other kings had served (Genesis 14:1, 4).

prompt: Who was the king of Salem and priest of God Most High who met Abram?
correct_answer: Melchizedek
explanation: Melchizedek, the king of Salem, met Abram and blessed him (Genesis 14:18).

prompt: Who was the king of Sodom mentioned in Genesis 14?
correct_answer: Bera
explanation: Bera was the king of Sodom who went to war against Chedorlaomer (Genesis 14:2).

prompt: According to Genesis 14, Melchizedek was a priest of whom?
correct_answer: God Most High
explanation: The text specifically titles him 'priest of God Most High' (Genesis 14:18).

prompt: How many years did the kings serve Chedorlaomer before they rebelled?
correct_answer: Twelve years
explanation: They served Chedorlaomer for twelve years and rebelled in the thirteenth (Genesis 14:4).

prompt: To what location did Abram pursue the defeated kings past Dan?
correct_answer: Hobah
explanation: Abram pursued them as far as Hobah, north of Damascus (Genesis 14:15).

prompt: What is another name for the Valley of Shaveh where the King of Sodom met Abram?
correct_answer: The King's Valley
explanation: The Valley of Shaveh is identified as the King's Valley (Genesis 14:17).

prompt: What tactic did Abram use to defeat the four kings at night?
choices_json: He used fire to panic them
He waited for them to sleep
correct_answer: He divided his forces
explanation: Abram divided his servants against them by night to strike them (Genesis 14:15).

prompt: What was the Valley of Siddim full of, which caused trouble for the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah?
correct_answer: Tar pits
explanation: The valley was full of tar pits (bitumen pits), and some fell in while fleeing (Genesis 14:10).

prompt: Where was Abram living at the time he heard about Lot's capture?
correct_answer: By the oaks of Mamre
explanation: Abram was living by the oaks of Mamre the Amorite (Genesis 14:13).

prompt: Who informed Abram the Hebrew that Lot had been captured?
correct_answer: One who had escaped
explanation: An escapee from the battle came and told Abram about Lot's capture (Genesis 14:13).

prompt: Who were the three allies of Abram who went with him to battle?
correct_answer: Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre
explanation: These three Amorite brothers were allies of Abram and joined the pursuit (Genesis 14:13, 24).

prompt: Why did Abram refuse to take even a thread or a sandal strap from the King of Sodom?
choices_json: He wanted no reward for war
Lest the king say he made Abram rich
correct_answer: Lest the king say he made Abram rich
explanation: Abram made an oath to ensure God alone received the credit for his wealth (Genesis 14:23).

prompt: How does Abram's behavior toward the King of Sodom demonstrate his integrity?
choices_json: He shared all the spoils equally
He refused personal gain to avoid ungodly obligations
He demanded the king convert to his faith
He gave the king a tenth of his own goods
correct_answer: He refused personal gain to avoid ungodly obligations
explanation: Abram's refusal of the spoils shows his reliance on God's promise rather than political favors (Genesis 14:22-23).

prompt: What distinguishes Abram's 'trained men' from a typical mercenary army?
choices_json: They were born in his own house
correct_answer: They were born in his own house
explanation: These men were part of Abram's extended household, showing his influence and preparation (Genesis 14:14).

prompt: What does Melchizedek's blessing of Abram highlight about God's role in the victory?
choices_json: God Most High delivered the enemies into Abram's hand
God favored the king of Sodom
The victory was due only to Abram's skill
correct_answer: God Most High delivered the enemies into Abram's hand
explanation: Melchizedek specifically credits God Most High with the victory (Genesis 14:20).

prompt: What does Melchizedek's title 'King of Salem' imply in the context of the Hebrew language?
correct_answer: King of Peace
explanation: Salem is derived from the word for peace ('shalom') (Genesis 14:18).

prompt: What does the phrase 'possessor of heaven and earth' describe about God in Melchizedek's blessing?
choices_json: His total sovereignty over all creation
His focus only on the afterlife
His role as a military advisor
correct_answer: His total sovereignty over all creation
explanation: The title emphasizes that everything in the physical and spiritual realms belongs to Him (Genesis 14:19).

prompt: What is the significance of the Valley of Siddim being identified as the Salt Sea?
choices_json: It was a major shipping port
It identifies the location for later readers as the Dead Sea
It explains why the land was fertile
It was the source of Abram's wealth
correct_answer: It identifies the location for later readers as the Dead Sea
explanation: The parenthetical note 'this is the Salt Sea' clarifies the geography (Genesis 14:3).

prompt: What theological concept is introduced through Abram giving a tenth to Melchizedek?
correct_answer: The concept of the tithing
explanation: This is one of the earliest biblical examples of giving a tenth to a priest (Genesis 14:20).

prompt: What was the purpose of the King of Sodom's offer to Abram: 'Give me the people, and take the goods for yourself'?
choices_json: To pay a debt of honor
To recover his subjects while rewarding the victor
To trick Abram into a trap
correct_answer: To recover his subjects while rewarding the victor
explanation: The king was willing to let Abram keep the plunder in exchange for the return of his people (Genesis 14:21).

prompt: Why did Abram specify that Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre should still take their portion?
choices_json: He recognized their rights despite his own personal vow
They demanded it in a contract
The king of Sodom insisted on it
correct_answer: He recognized their rights despite his own personal vow
explanation: Abram didn't impose his own ascetic choice on his allies (Genesis 14:24).

prompt: Why did the four kings attack groups like the Rephaim and Zuzim before reaching the five kings?
choices_json: To clear the route and suppress potential allies
To find water for their armies
correct_answer: To clear the route and suppress potential allies
explanation: Their campaign systematically struck the surrounding tribes to consolidate control (Genesis 14:5-7).

prompt: How does Abram's rescue of the women and other people (v. 16) expand his role in the story?
choices_json: He acts as a redeemer for more than just his own family
He is looking for new wives
He is building a larger army for himself
correct_answer: He acts as a redeemer for more than just his own family
explanation: Abram's victory brought liberty to all the captives of Sodom, not just Lot (Genesis 14:16).

prompt: How does Abram's treatment of his allies' portion (v. 24) reflect his character?
choices_json: He was greedy for their share
He was fair and respected the rights of those who labored with him
He was trying to buy their loyalty
He was afraid they would turn on him
correct_answer: He was fair and respected the rights of those who labored with him
explanation: Abram balanced his own spiritual convictions with justice for his partners (Genesis 14:24).

prompt: How does the designation of Abram as 'the Hebrew' in Genesis 14:13 function in the narrative?
choices_json: It identifies him as an outsider or foreigner to the local kings
correct_answer: It identifies him as an outsider or foreigner to the local kings
explanation: It distinguishes Abram from the warring city-state kings of the region (Genesis 14:13).

prompt: In the battle of the kings, how does the 'four against five' detail contribute to the story's theme?
choices_json: The smaller alliance of four was surprisingly more powerful and disciplined
Numerical superiority is the only thing that matters in Genesis
The kings of Sodom were superior tacticians
correct_answer: The smaller alliance of four was surprisingly more powerful and disciplined
explanation: The four eastern kings successfully defeated the five local kings despite being outnumbered in kings (Genesis 14:9).

prompt: In what way does the rescue of Lot contrast with Abram's experience in Egypt (Genesis 12)?
choices_json: In Egypt he was a warrior, here he is a diplomat
In Egypt he relied on deception, here he acts with bold military strength
Both stories end with Abram losing his wealth
There is no contrast between the two stories
correct_answer: In Egypt he relied on deception, here he acts with bold military strength
explanation: Genesis 14 shows a more assertive and powerful side of Abram compared to the Egypt incident (Genesis 14:14-16).

prompt: What does Abram's pursuit of the kings to 'Dan' suggest about the text's composition?
choices_json: The city was always called Dan
It may be a later editorial update for readers familiar with the tribal territory
correct_answer: It may be a later editorial update for readers familiar with the tribal territory
explanation: The territory was named Dan much later, suggesting the name was used to help later readers identify the location (Genesis 14:14).

prompt: What is the meaning of Abram 'lifting up his hand' to the LORD?
choices_json: A formal oath or vow taken before God
A sign for his men to attack
A way to ward off evil
correct_answer: A formal oath or vow taken before God
explanation: Lifting the hand was a symbolic act of swearing a solemn promise (Genesis 14:22).

prompt: What is the narrative significance of the meeting with Melchizedek occurring before the discussion with the King of Sodom?
choices_json: It grounds Abram's success in divine blessing before he faces a worldly temptation
It shows Melchizedek was more powerful than Bera
It confirms the war is not yet over
correct_answer: It grounds Abram's success in divine blessing before he faces a worldly temptation
explanation: The spiritual encounter prepares Abram to handle the King of Sodom's offer with integrity (Genesis 14:18-21).

prompt: What role does the 'one who had escaped' (v. 13) play in the narrative?
choices_json: He serves as a bridge between the regional war and Abram's personal life
He is a spy for Chedorlaomer
He is a relative of Melchizedek
correct_answer: He serves as a bridge between the regional war and Abram's personal life
explanation: The messenger brings the news that forces Abram to move from observer to participant (Genesis 14:13).

prompt: Why is the list of defeated tribes like the Zuzim and Emim included in the account of Chedorlaomer's march?
choices_json: To show they were Abram's enemies
To illustrate the sheer scale and devastation of the eastern kings' campaign
To explain where Abram got his soldiers
To provide a genealogy for Melchizedek
correct_answer: To illustrate the sheer scale and devastation of the eastern kings' campaign
explanation: The exhaustive list shows that no one could stand against the alliance until they met Abram (Genesis 14:5-7).

prompt: How does Abram's victory over the eastern kings relate to the promises made in Genesis 12?
choices_json: It contradicts the promise of peace
It demonstrates that Abram would be a source of blessing and protection to others
It shows Abram was supposed to be a conqueror of nations
It was a distraction from the main promise
correct_answer: It demonstrates that Abram would be a source of blessing and protection to others
explanation: By rescuing Lot and the captives, Abram begins to fulfill the promise that 'in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed' (Genesis 12:3; 14:16).

prompt: How does the capture of Lot 'Abram's brother's son' emphasize a key narrative tension in Genesis?
choices_json: Lot's constant need for rescue despite his choice to live in a wicked city
The fact that Lot was the real hero
The lack of communication between them
correct_answer: Lot's constant need for rescue despite his choice to live in a wicked city

prompt: How is Melchizedek utilized in later biblical theology, particularly in the book of Hebrews?
choices_json: As a type of Christ, a priest-king with an eternal priesthood
As a warning against local kings
As a minor figure with no further importance
As the literal father of Abram
correct_answer: As a type of Christ, a priest-king with an eternal priesthood
explanation: Hebrews 7 draws extensively on Genesis 14 to explain the nature of Christ's priesthood (Hebrews 7:1-3).

prompt: The Valley of Siddim battle is the first recorded war in the Bible; what does its inclusion establish?
choices_json: The political volatility of the land into which Abram has moved
That Abram was a king from the beginning
The end of the book of Genesis
correct_answer: The political volatility of the land into which Abram has moved

prompt: The name 'El Elyon' (God Most High) used by Melchizedek serves what theological purpose?
choices_json: To name a local mountain god
To assert God's supremacy over all other claims to divinity or power
To distinguish between the God of the Jews and Gentiles
To refer specifically to the moon god
correct_answer: To assert God's supremacy over all other claims to divinity or power
explanation: The title declares that the God Abram serves is the ultimate authority over the whole world (Genesis 14:19).

prompt: What does Abram's insistence on paying his allies fairly (v. 24) reveal about the social ethics of the time?
choices_json: That individual vows did not negate common justice or property rights
That the King of Sodom owned all allies
That Abram was trying to avoid a lawsuit
correct_answer: That individual vows did not negate common justice or property rights

prompt: What does the phrase 'left hand of Damascus' (v. 15) mean in ancient geographic orientation?
correct_answer: North of Damascus
explanation: In ancient Hebrew orientation, 'left' corresponded to the North (Genesis 14:15).

prompt: What is the contrast between Melchizedek's blessing and the King of Sodom's offer?
choices_json: One is spiritual and life-giving, the other is transactional and potentially compromising

Melchizedek is a threat, the king is a friend

prompt: What is the significance of the bread and wine brought by Melchizedek in later Christian tradition?
choices_json: A sign of a common meal
A precursor or 'type' of the elements used in the Eucharist
correct_answer: A precursor or 'type' of the elements used in the Eucharist
explanation: The bread and wine are seen as symbolic of the New Covenant elements brought by the ultimate High Priest (Genesis 14:18).

prompt: Why is Melchizedek's lack of genealogy in Genesis 14 significant for later interpretation?

It proves he was a king of the Gentiles only
correct_answer: It allows him to represent a priesthood not based on human descent

prompt: According to Genesis 15:13, how long would Abram's offspring be afflicted in a foreign land?

prompt: From which location did Yahweh say He brought Abram out to give him the land?

prompt: In Genesis 15:1, what does Yahweh tell Abram He is to him?

correct_answer: A shield and an exceedingly great reward
explanation: Yahweh tells Abram, 'Don't be afraid, Abram. I am your shield, your exceedingly great reward' (Genesis 15:1).

prompt: What did Abram do when birds of prey came down on the carcasses?

correct_answer: The stars

prompt: What happened to Abram as the sun was going down in Genesis 15:12?
choices_json: A deep sleep fell on him
correct_answer: A deep sleep fell on him
explanation: The text says, 'When the sun was going down, a deep sleep fell on Abram' (Genesis 15:12).

prompt: What passed between the divided animal pieces in Abram's vision?

A smoking furnace and a flaming torch

He would die in the foreign land

prompt: Which of these birds was Abram instructed to bring for the sacrifice in Genesis 15?

prompt: How old were the heifer, goat, and ram that Abram was told to bring?
correct_answer: Three years old

He would be a servant born in his house
He who will come out of Abram's own body
correct_answer: He who will come out of Abram's own body

correct_answer: How will I know that I will inherit it?

prompt: What did Yahweh say his offspring would bring with them when they eventually left the land of their affliction?

He drove them away from the carcasses

correct_answer: The great river Euphrates

prompt: According to the specific list of nations in Genesis 15:19-21, which tribe is listed immediately before the Girgashites?
correct_answer: Canaanites

prompt: How does Genesis 15 describe the relationship between faith and righteousness?

Righteousness is only for the offspring of Abram

prompt: In the Hebrew context of the 'divided pieces,' what was the symbolic significance of the smoking furnace and flaming torch?

prompt: The 'iniquity of the Amorite' not being 'full' suggests what about God's character in this chapter?

God is unable to remove the Amorites yet

prompt: What is the historical significance of the 'River of Egypt' in the land grant to Abram?
choices_json: It is another name for the Nile
It represents the border between the promised land and Egypt
It is a river that no longer exists
